1: The resumption formula ve'atah ben-adam hinabu al-Gog (and you, son of man, prophesy against Gog) reopens the oracle of Ezekiel 38. The two-chapter unit (38-39) forms the great Gog oracle — Ezekiel's eschatological battle text. Chapter 38 announced the invasion; chapter 39 describes YHWH's destruction of Gog and its theological aftermath. Rosh nasi Meshech veTuval (chief prince of Meshech and Tubal) — rosh here is a title (chief/head), not the country Russia (a common anachronistic misreading; the connection to Russia comes from 19th-century exegetes reading Rosh as a proper name).
2: veshivvathicha veshishethicha: 'I will turn you back and lead you on.' The Hebrew roots here are debated: shavah can mean 'to turn back' or 'to lead,' and shashah can mean 'to lead' or 'to pull along by a hook' (cf. 38:4 where YHWH puts hooks in Gog's jaws). The MKT renders both verbs actively — YHWH is the agent who drives Gog forward to Israel's mountains. The crucial theological point is that Gog's invasion is not successful enemy initiative but YHWH's own deployment of an enemy instrument, as with Assyria in Isaiah 10:5-6.
3: vehikeithi qashtech miyad semolecha: 'I will strike your bow from your left hand.' Military disarmament by divine action — the bow (qashet) from the left hand and arrows (chitzim) from the right hand. Archery was the dominant long-range weapon of ancient Near Eastern warfare. YHWH's direct action against Gog's weapons mirrors the exodus tradition: he fights for Israel, not Israel fighting for itself. The stripping of weapons prefigures the subsequent burning of those weapons by Israel over seven years (Ezekiel 39:9-10).
4: al-harei Yisrael tipol atah vechal-agapeicha: 'On the mountains of Israel you will fall, you and all your troops.' The mountains of Israel (harei Yisrael) is a recurring Ezekielian location that appears in both judgment oracles against Israel (Ezekiel 6) and salvation oracles for Israel (Ezekiel 36). Gog falls in Israel's own land — judgment executed where the enemy had anticipated victory. Lefzit qin'ah (to the birds of prey of every kind) — the feeding of enemies to birds and beasts is the reversal of proper burial, a form of judgment that denies the defeated the dignity of covenant burial (cf. Deuteronomy 28:26).
5: al-penei hasadeh tipol: 'You will fall in the open field.' The sadeh (open field, countryside) burial-denial formula is sealed with ki ani dibarti ne'um adonai YHWH (for I have spoken, declares the Lord YHWH). Ezekiel's characteristic concluding formula makes the divine speech itself the guarantee of the outcome — not military analysis or political prediction but the performative word of YHWH that accomplishes what it announces.
6: veshilachti esh beMagog: 'I will send fire on Magog.' The judgment extends beyond Gog's army to Magog (the homeland) and the coastlands (iyyim) that “dwell in security.” Esh (fire) as the instrument of judgment is YHWH's characteristic weapon (Isaiah 30:30; Amos 1-2; Revelation 20:9). The recognition formula veyad'u ki ani YHWH (they will know that I am YHWH) extends to the nations: the Gog defeat is not just Israel's salvation but a universal theophany that makes YHWH known among all peoples.
7: ve'et-shem qodshi odiya bekerev ami Yisrael: 'I will make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel.' Shem qodshi (my holy name) is the theological center of the Gog oracle and of Ezekiel's entire theology: YHWH's purpose is the vindication of his name among nations (cf. Ezekiel 36:20-23, where Israel's exile had profaned YHWH's name among the nations). The Gog defeat is the eschatological event that restores YHWH's reputation — the nations will know that Israel's exile was not YHWH's defeat but his judgment, and Israel's restoration is his power. Lo akhallel et-shem qodshi od (I will not let my holy name be profaned anymore) — the permanent securing of the divine honor.
8: hineh baat umayom asher dibarti: 'Behold it is coming and it will happen, declares the Lord YHWH. This is the day of which I have spoken.' Ba'ah (it is coming) as perfect tense — prophetic certainty. The phrase zeh hayom asher dibarti (this is the day of which I have spoken) connects the Gog oracle to YHWH's prior speech — possibly to the restoration oracles of Ezekiel 34-37 or to the foundational threat oracles of Ezekiel 4-24. The day is singular — hayom — but it encompasses the eschatological event Ezekiel has been building toward.
9: veyatze'u yoshevei arei Yisrael uviy'aru vehissiqu bensheq: 'Those who dwell in the cities of Israel will go out and make fires of the weapons.' The burning of weapons for seven years (sheva shanim) is the practical consequence of total military victory. Magen vetzinnah qeshet vachitzim umes madanot varomach — the weapon list (shields, bucklers, bow, arrows, war clubs, spears) represents the complete arsenal of ancient Near Eastern warfare. Using enemy weapons as fuel rather than importing wood from the forest is a sign of the complete sufficiency of the victory — the defeated enemy's equipment meets all of Israel's energy needs.
10: lo yis'u etzim min-hasadeh: 'They shall not carry wood from the field.' The fuel self-sufficiency of Ezekiel 39:9-10 has a symbolic dimension beyond practicality: Israel does not need to go out to the territory (sadeh) where enemies dwell to gather their sustenance. The plundering of those who plundered them and the spoiling of those who spoiled them (veshaleloo et-sholeheihem ve'vezzoo et-bozezehem) is the classic prophetic reversal — exact recompense through divine governance of history.
11: venathati leGog makom sham qever beYisrael: 'I will give Gog a burial place there in Israel.' Gei ha-avarim (Valley of the Travelers/Passers-by) — the name is debated: avarim can mean “those who pass through” (common travelers) or is related to the Abarim mountains in Transjordan (Numbers 27:12; Deuteronomy 32:49). The MKT renders it 'travelers' following the contextual use. The renamed valley Gei Hamon-Gog (Valley of the Multitude of Gog) commemorates permanently the scale of YHWH's victory. The nose-blocking of the passers-by (ve'gosemeth et-avar ha-avarim) indicates an enormous stench from the mass of corpses.
12: uvekabram otam beit Yisrael: 'The house of Israel will be burying them.' Seven months (shiva chodeshim) of burial is the temporal counterpart to the seven years of weapon-burning (Ezekiel 39:9) — the number seven in both cases signals completeness. The extended burial process communicates the scale of Gog's defeat but also the thoroughness of the land's cleansing: Israel must actively participate in removing all traces of the hostile army from the land of covenant blessing.
13: vekabru kol-am haarets: 'All the people of the land will bury them.' The communal burial is characterized as a source of honor (ve'hayah lahem leshem = it will be to them a name/renown) in the day YHWH glorifies himself (beyom hikavedi). The burying of the enemy is not merely hygienic but theological — it is participation in YHWH's great act of vindication. The phrase beyom hikavedi (on the day I am glorified/honored) connects the burial directly to the kavod (glory) theme that runs through Ezekiel's theology.
14: va'anshei tamid yavdilu oveerei baaretz: 'Men shall be set apart, regularly employed to pass through the land.' Anshei tamid (men of continualness/regularity) — a specialized burial detail (mekabrim) whose continual task is to search the land for remaining bones. The thoroughness of the land-cleansing operation emphasizes that even scattered and forgotten bones must be located. The passage of seven months was not sufficient — a dedicated professional corps is needed for the final purification.
15: verau etzem adam: 'And when any one sees a human bone.' The wayfarer who sees a bone sets up a marker (tziyyun = a road marker, sign) beside it so that the burial detail can find it. This is the ancient equivalent of a danger marker and collection protocol. The systematic nature of the cleanup — specialized workers, markers, final collection — communicates that land defilement by unburied corpses (cf. Numbers 19:16) has cosmic importance: the land must be ritually pure for the divine presence to return (Ezekiel 40-48).
16: vegam shem-ir Hamonah: 'And the name of the city will also be Hamonah.' Hamonah (multitude, horde) is the city near the burial valley that will bear the name as a permanent memorial of YHWH's victory. The naming of both the valley (Hamon-gog, Ezekiel 39:11) and the city (Hamonah) permanently commemorates the defeat — the landscape itself becomes memorial testimony. This anticipates the eschatological transformation of place-names in Ezekiel's vision (Ezekiel 44:35: Jerusalem named “YHWH is There”).
17: ve'atah ben-adam koh amar adonai YHWH emor letzipor kol-kanaf: 'And you, son of man, thus says the Lord YHWH: Speak to every winged bird.' The summoning of birds and beasts to the sacrificial feast on Gog's corpses is deliberately described as a zevach (sacrifice) and a mishteh (feast/drinking party). The feast on the mighty and their horses (Ezekiel 39:18-20) is the inverse of the covenant sacrificial feast — instead of Israel feasting on covenant blessing, the creation feasts on covenant-enemy. Revelation 19:17-18 quotes this passage for the eschatological battle's aftermath.
18: bashar gibborim tochelu vedam nesiayei haaretz tishteh: 'You will eat the flesh of mighty men and drink the blood of princes.' The specifics — alim (rams), karim (lambs), attudim (he-goats), parim (bulls) — use sacrificial animal terminology metaphorically for human rulers and warriors. The princes (nesiyim), mighty men (gibborim), and their forces are the “animals” at this inverted feast. The drinking of blood — normally the ultimate dietary prohibition in Israel (Leviticus 17:14) — here applied to birds and beasts signals that this feast is outside all normal categories: it is YHWH's own slaughter-feast.
19: va'akaltem chelev lesova: 'You will eat fat until you are satisfied.' The three words — chelev (fat, the richest portions), lesova (to satiety/fullness), ve'shatem dam lishkarah (and drink blood to drunkenness) — are the language of excessive feasting. The irony is that the fat and blood — both reserved for YHWH in the sacrificial system (Leviticus 3:16-17) — are here given to creation's scavengers, because this feast is YHWH's own judgment-sacrifice in which the enemy army is the animal offering.
20: usevitem al-shulchani: 'You will be filled at my table.' YHWH's table (shulchani) at which birds and beasts feast is the judgment-altar's counterpart to the sanctuary table (the table of the Presence). The complete military taxonomy — horses, riders, mighty men, warriors — confirms the universality of the judgment: no component of Gog's force escapes. The recognition formula follows: venathati et-kevodi bagoyim (I will set my glory among the nations).
21: venathati et-kevodi bagoyim: 'I will set my glory among the nations.' The kavod (glory) statement is the theological climax of the Gog narrative. YHWH's purpose throughout the oracle has been the universal recognition of his sovereign power — not merely Israelite vindication but cosmic theophany. Vereyu kol-hagoyim et-mishpati (all the nations will see my judgment) — the Gog battle is a public event visible to all peoples, establishing beyond doubt that YHWH governs history and judges those who challenge his covenant purposes.
22: veyad'u beit Yisrael ki ani YHWH elohehem: 'And the house of Israel will know that I am YHWH their God.' The recognition formula is applied to Israel specifically here: elohehem (their God) adds the covenant dimension — not merely that YHWH exists or is powerful but that he is Israel's own covenant God who has acted on their behalf. The phrase min-hayom hahu vema'alah (from that day onward) signals that the Gog defeat produces a permanent change in Israel's covenant knowledge — no more forgetting, no more idol substitution.
23: veyad'u hagoyim ki ba'avonam galu beit Yisrael: 'The nations will know that the house of Israel went into exile for their iniquity.' The theological explanation of the exile for the benefit of the nations: YHWH hid his face (hisstarti panai) not because he was weak but because Israel sinned (be'avonam). YHWH's hiding of his face was a judicial act, not a defeat. The nations' misunderstanding of the exile (assuming YHWH was powerless to prevent it) is now corrected by the restoration — they learn both why Israel was exiled and why YHWH is now acting for her.
24: ketumatam uchefishehem asiti otam: 'According to their impurity and according to their transgressions I acted against them.' The theological vindication is precise: YHWH's action in exile was perfectly calibrated to Israel's sin. Tuma (impurity, ritual/ethical defilement) and pasha (transgression, covenant violation) are the two categories of Israel's failure. YHWH's judicial response was measured, not arbitrary or excessive. The nations can now see that the exile was proportionate divine justice, not random military misfortune.
25: lachan koh amar adonai YHWH atah ashiv et-shevut Yaakov: 'Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Now I will restore the fortunes of Jacob.' The lachan (therefore) of restoration is the climactic reversal. Shevut Yaakov (the fortunes of Jacob) — the formula shav shevut signals the turning of captivity/fortune (it can mean both restoring from exile and reversing misfortune). The jealousy for YHWH's holy name (qinati leshemi qodshi) is the motivation — the same jealousy that motivated judgment (Ezekiel 36:5-6) now motivates restoration. Divine jealousy is not petty but the covenant passion that drives both justice and redemption.
26: venessu et-kelimmatam vekhol-ma'alam asher ma'alu vi: 'They will bear their shame and all their treachery with which they have been unfaithful to me.' The restored Israel will not be returned to complacency — they will bear (nasa = to carry, to forgive, to bear responsibility for) the memory of their shame. This is neither condemnation nor forgotten grace: the shame is borne in the context of security (beshivtam al-admatam labetach = dwelling securely on their land). Held shame in the context of security is the formative posture of those who have experienced grace they did not deserve.
27: beshofeti otam beshivti et-shevutam: 'When I have gathered them from the peoples and gathered them from their enemies' lands.' The public kiddush hashem (sanctification of the divine name) executed before many nations — venikdashti bam le'einei goyim rabbim (I will manifest my holiness through them before many nations). The return of the exiles is itself a theophanic event: it declares to watching nations that YHWH is who he claimed to be. The restoration is not merely humanitarian but doxological — its purpose is the universal proclamation of YHWH's holiness.
28: veyad'u ki ani YHWH elohehem begaloti otam el-hagoyim: 'They will know that I am YHWH their God — I who exiled them among the nations.' The theological conclusion states explicitly what the narrative has implied throughout: YHWH was the agent of both exile and restoration. The same God who exiled (galoti otam = I exiled them) now gathers (vikibatzti otam al-admatam = I gathered them to their land). Divine sovereignty governs both movements — the scattering and the ingathering are both YHWH's actions, which is the basis of hope. Velo-otir od mehם (and I left none of them behind) — complete restoration, not a remnant of a remnant.
29: velo-astir od panai mehem: 'I will no longer hide my face from them.' The reversal of the judicial face-hiding (hisstarti panai of Ezekiel 39:23-24) is the ultimate restoration promise. Asher shafachti et-ruachi al-beit Yisrael (when I have poured out my Spirit on the house of Israel) — the Spirit-pouring is both the means and the sign of the restoration. The poured-out Spirit is the eschatological fulfillment that Joel 2:28-32 develops and Acts 2:17-21 applies to Pentecost. The final seal: ne'um adonai YHWH — the divine declaration that closes the great Gog oracle.
